cues.speaker: Ben Purslow
cues.text: Thanks for pulling these benchmarks together, Maria. Let's walk through what you found.

cues.speaker: Maria Santos
cues.text: Sure. I ran our three core query patterns against Postgres and MongoDB on identical hardware, with realistic seed data.

cues.speaker: Maria Santos
cues.text: The big finding is that our task and project queries hit Postgres significantly faster once joins come into play.

cues.speaker: Ben Purslow
cues.text: How significant are we talking, in practice?

cues.speaker: Maria Santos
cues.text: On the dashboard query, Postgres returned in about thirty milliseconds. Mongo was closer to one hundred and ten.

cues.speaker: Maria Santos
cues.text: On the assignment lookup it was tighter, but Postgres still came in roughly twice as fast.

cues.speaker: Ben Purslow
cues.text: And that's with reasonable indexes on both sides? Not a stacked deck?

cues.speaker: Maria Santos
cues.text: Yes. I added compound indexes on the Mongo collections to match what we'd realistically deploy.

cues.speaker: Maria Santos
cues.text: The relational shape of the data is just a better fit for Postgres.

cues.speaker: Ben Purslow
cues.text: Right, that tracks with what I'd expected.

cues.speaker: Ben Purslow
cues.text: And from an infrastructure point of view, Tom's already confirmed Postgres slots into what we're running today.

cues.speaker: Maria Santos
cues.text: Right. Backups, monitoring, and migration tooling all assume Postgres in our existing stack.

cues.speaker: Maria Santos
cues.text: Mongo would mean a second set of operational playbooks, and we don't have the bandwidth to build those right now.

cues.speaker: Ben Purslow
cues.text: Are there any features Mongo gives us that we'd actually miss?

cues.speaker: Maria Santos
cues.text: Schema flexibility, mainly. But our data model isn't moving fast enough to need it.

cues.speaker: Maria Santos
cues.text: And Postgres jsonb covers the cases where we genuinely need unstructured fields.

cues.speaker: Ben Purslow
cues.text: Good. We've used jsonb for audit logs before without issues.

cues.speaker: Ben Purslow
cues.text: What about team familiarity? I want to make sure we're not picking something only one person can operate.

cues.speaker: Maria Santos
cues.text: Three of us have shipped Postgres in production. Only Tom has any meaningful Mongo experience.

cues.speaker: Maria Santos
cues.text: And he flagged earlier that his Mongo experience is two years out of date now.

cues.speaker: Ben Purslow
cues.text: Okay, that's a fair point to flag.

cues.speaker: Ben Purslow
cues.text: Any concerns about scale? Are we going to hit a wall with Postgres in the first year?

cues.speaker: Maria Santos
cues.text: Not at our projected volumes. We'd be well under a million rows on the largest table in year one.

cues.speaker: Maria Santos
cues.text: Read replicas and connection pooling will get us comfortably to ten times that if we need it.

cues.speaker: Ben Purslow
cues.text: Good. I think the answer here is pretty clear, then.

cues.speaker: Ben Purslow
cues.text: Let's lock in Postgres as the primary store. Can you write up the decision and the benchmark summary?

cues.speaker: Maria Santos
cues.text: Yes, I'll have it out today.

cues.speaker: Maria Santos
cues.text: And I'll coordinate with Tom to get the staging instance and connection config documented this week.

cues.speaker: Ben Purslow
cues.text: Perfect. Anything else we need to flag before we move on?

cues.speaker: Maria Santos
cues.text: Just that the schema migration tool decision is still open. I'd like to scope that separately next week.
